confirm.message: Start a new game? Current progress will be lost.

items.title: Objective
items.desc: Fill the 9×9 grid with digits 1~9 so that each row, each column, and each 3×3 box contains all digits 1~9 exactly once.

items.title: Three Constraints
items.desc: No repeated digits in any row, column, or 3×3 box. The diagram below highlights the three constraint regions in different colors.

items.title: Naked Single
items.desc: If a cell's row, column, and box already contain 8 different digits, the remaining digit is the only possibility. The blue cell in the diagram must be 9.

items.title: Hidden Single
items.desc:
In a row/column/box, one of the digits 1~9 can only fit in a single cell. Even if other candidates exist, the answer is determined.

Look at the board below: Row 1 needs a 6, but every column already has a 6 except column 5. So Row 1, Column 5 must be 6 — this is a Hidden Single.

items.title: Box-Line Reduction
items.desc:
When a digit in a row can only appear within a single box, that digit can be eliminated from other cells in that box.

In the diagram: Row 3 needs a 7, but columns 4~9 already have 7s in other rows (red cross marks). Columns 5 and 7 are already filled. So 7 can only go in columns 1~3 (top-left box, purple highlights). → Cells in the top-left box that are NOT in row 3 cannot have 7 (red cross marks).

items.title: Naked Pair
items.desc:
When two cells in the same row/column/box contain only the same two candidates, those digits can be eliminated from other cells in that unit.

In the diagram: Two orange cells in Row 4 form a ｛2,7｝ naked pair. The blue cell in Column 3 originally had candidates ｛2,3,7｝. After eliminating 2 and 7, only 3 remains — solution found!

items.title: Naked Triple
items.desc: An extension of Naked Pair. If three cells together contain only three unique candidates, those digits can be eliminated from other cells in that unit. The orange cells form a ｛1,4,9｝ naked triple in the diagram.

items.title: X-Wing
items.desc: When a digit appears in only two columns across two rows, that digit can be eliminated from those columns in other rows, forming an X shape. In the diagram, digit 3 in rows 1 and 7 can only go in columns 3 and 6.

items.title: XY-Wing
items.desc: Three cells A{X,Y}, B{X,Z}, C{Y,Z} form an XY-Wing. Cells that see both B and C cannot be Z. In the diagram, A(1,5), B(1,7), C(4,5) form an XY-Wing, eliminating 8 from cell (4,7).

items.title: Chain Reasoning
items.desc: "If A is X, then a contradiction follows, so A must be Y." Used for the hardest expert puzzles, usually requiring manual candidate tracking.
